figures.gloss: The sky-king of the Etruscan triad, hurler of three grades of thunderbolt — one he casts at will, two only with the counsel of the veiled gods. Syncretised with Jupiter and Zeus once Rome absorbed his people.
figures.facet: A sky-father who is NOT sovereign over the lightning: he must take counsel before the gravest bolts. The Etruscan shard shows the storm-king bound by a council — even the highest god obeys a higher procedure.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from Seneca and Pliny on Etruscan fulgural doctrine; the council of the dii consentes is attested but its membership debated.

figures.gloss: Queen-consort of Tinia and tutelary mother of the city of Veii, identified by the Etruscans themselves with Juno and with Punic Astarte on the Pyrgi Tablets — a goddess of sovereignty and the suckling breast.
figures.provenance: The Pyrgi gold tablets (c. 500 BCE) equate Uni with Phoenician Astarte in a bilingual dedication — a documented act of self-syncretism.

figures.gloss: Maiden goddess of wisdom, crafts and the thunderbolt, third of the great Etruscan triad. The Romans received her as Minerva and through her the Greek Athena, but the Etruscans gave her lightning the Greeks never did.
figures.facet: A wisdom-goddess who wields the bolt: in Etruria Menrva is herself a thunder-caster, a feminine claim on the storm-weapon that her Greek and Roman cognates surrendered to the sky-father.

figures.gloss: Lord of the Etruscan dead, wolf-capped and grim, enthroned beside his queen Phersipnai in the painted tombs of Tarquinia and Orvieto. His very name and image are borrowed from Greek Hades.
figures.provenance: Aita (Eita) appears late, a hellenised form layered over older native death-powers; the wolf-cap is the Etruscan signature added to the Greek loan.

figures.gloss: Queen of the Etruscan underworld, consort of Aita, her name a direct Etruscan rendering of Greek Persephone — the grain-maiden made dread sovereign of the tomb.
figures.provenance: Attested in tomb paintings (Tomba dell'Orco II, Tarquinia); a transparent borrowing of Persephone's name and station.

figures.gloss: Winged female demon of death, torch and scroll in hand, who attends the dying and escorts souls without malice — she does not judge or torment, she witnesses and conducts.
figures.facet: A psychopomp with no analogue in the Greek loan-set: Vanth is native Etruscan, a benign winged escort with eyes upon her wings, all-seeing, who marks the appointed hour rather than punishing the dead.
figures.provenance: Purely Etruscan iconography, no Greek prototype; paired with the hammer-demon Charun in tomb art.

figures.gloss: Blue-fleshed, tusked, hammer-bearing guardian of the death-passage, who strikes the threshold of the tomb. He shares Greek Charon's name but not his trade — no ferry, but a mallet.
figures.facet: The name is Charon's; the office is not. Where the Greek poles a boat across a river, the Etruscan Charun seals the door of death with a hammer-blow — a borrowed name fitted to a wholly native demon.

figures.gloss: A bronze model of a sheep's liver divided into forty regions, sixteen on the rim, each inscribed with a god's name — a map of the templum, the quartered heaven projected onto the organ so the haruspex could read the will of the sky in the body of the sacrifice.
figures.facet: The cosmos as a divination-grid: the Etruscan templum quarters the sky into sixteen houses of gods, an axis-and-quarters scheme read not in stars but in flesh — the only such bronze cosmogram to survive from the ancient Mediterranean.
figures.provenance: The Piacenza Liver (2nd c. BCE bronze); the sixteen-fold sky is corroborated by Martianus Capella's later list of the regiones caeli.

figures.gloss: The revealed science of reading divine will — haruspicy (the liver), fulgural lore (lightning), and the brontoscopic calendar (thunder by day) — held to be dictated by the child-sage Tages and the nymph Vegoia, a fixed law the gods themselves announce.
figures.facet: Revelation as cosmic-order: the Etruscans alone systematised the reading of the divine into a written discipline with a doctrine of world-ages — order is not obeyed but DECODED, the future spelled out in liver, bolt and thunder.

figures.gloss: The child with an old man's wisdom who sprang from a freshly ploughed furrow at Tarquinia and dictated the whole disciplina to the assembled Etruscans before sinking back into the earth.
figures.facet: A culture-hero born from the furrow and unborn from it again in a single day: the bringer of the sacred sciences arrives not from the sky but UP from the tilled field, an earth-born teacher of the arts of reading heaven.
figures.provenance: Cicero (De Divinatione) and Ovid relate the Tages legend; a foundational Etruscan myth preserved only through Roman authors.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A rigorously guarded, highly codified graphic and ritual system for mapping divine pacts and unseen forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The reduction of divine communion to an exact, unvarying rhythmic science, where precise spoken formulas compel spiritual mechanics without room for ad-hoc petition.
GOLD SEAM: Chthonic female entities associated with the protection of boundaries, the deceased, and subterranean spaces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an elite, esoteric priesthood dedicated to observing precise celestial mechanics to divine and manage the volatile will of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly formalized, technical mechanisms of divination used to establish state law and construct religious architecture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An otherworldly being who arrives suddenly to teach a complex system of spiritual and practical wisdom to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a formalized, visually cataloged system of reading divine will through omens and drawn lots.
GOLD SEAM: A fiercely guarded, highly codified body of esoteric knowledge accessible only to a trained, ascetic elite.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a rigid, divinely revealed set of operating instructions meant to maintain the cosmic order through exact ritual compliance.
The Etruscans' own equation: Tinia is the model and donor for Roman Jupiter, the Capitoline triad (Tinia-Uni-Menrva → Jupiter-Juno-Minerva) being the most direct Etruscan bequest to Rome.
Through Greek contact and Roman mediation Tinia was assimilated to Zeus; his thunderbolt iconography is borrowed from Greek Zeus-imagery.
Consort-pair within the Etruscan triad, mirroring the Tinia-Uni sovereignty couple at the head of the pantheon.
Both are the sovereign queen-consort of the sky-king, tutelary mother of the people — a recurrent great-mother-beside-sky-father joint.
The Pyrgi Tablets equate Uni with Phoenician Astarte; the Astarte-Isis-Juno chain of Mediterranean great-goddess identification is documented in the syncretic age.
Third member of the Etruscan triad alongside Tinia and Uni — the wisdom-and-craft seat of the sovereign three.
Shared smith/craft archetype — Menrva patronises artisans as Hephaestus does, though she is also a wisdom-goddess.
Both join wisdom/craft to the thunder-weapon's making — Menrva wields the bolt, Tvastr forges Indra's; the smith near the storm.
Aita is a direct hellenised borrowing of Hades — name, throne and consort imported, the wolf-cap added as the native signature.
Independent underworld-sovereigns of unrelated traditions — both grim enthroned lords of a chthonic realm, convergence on the death-king joint.
Phersipnai is Persephone's name rendered into Etruscan — a transparent loan of the maiden-queen of the dead.
The Etruscan death-couple, modelled on the Greek Hades-Persephone pairing they imported together.
Charun shares Charon's name and underworld station but inverts the office — hammer for the ferry-pole; a borrowed name fitted to a native death-demon.
Where Charon takes payment to row the dead across, Charun seals the tomb-door with a mallet — the same name, the opposite gesture toward the threshold.
The native Etruscan death-pair of tomb-art: Vanth the benign winged witness, Charun the hammer-guardian, who attend the dying together.
Both are winged female death-attendants who mark rather than cause the appointed end — the Morrigan over the battlefield, Vanth at the deathbed.
Psychopomps who conduct without condemning — both escort the soul through the passage as office-holders of the threshold, not judges.
Two quartered-cosmos grids of unrelated peoples: the templum's sixteen sky-houses on the liver and the four-directions board of Patolli, the cosmos drawn as a divided field to be read or played.
The quartered heaven as a working diagram — sixteen regiones around the rim echoing the four-cornered cosmos held by the four Tezcatlipocas; the axis-and-quarters joint surfacing on two continents.
Both project a quartered, centred cosmos as sacred map — the templum divided into orienting regions as Eden's garden parts into four rivers from the centre.
The liver IS an instrument of the disciplina — haruspicy is one of its three branches, the bronze model its surviving textbook in metal. (Same tradition: an internal structural seam.)
Both posit an impersonal right-order the cosmos announces and the wise decode — the Dao read in the cosmos, the disciplina read in liver, bolt and thunder.
Comparable conceptions of an all-pervading sacred order made legible through ritual specialists — the Etruscan revealed-law beside the Nahua teotl as ground of the read cosmos.
Culture-heroes who hand humankind the saving arts — Prometheus brings fire and craft, Tages brings the sciences of reading the divine; the benefactor face of the bringer-of-arts.
Both are teacher-heroes who deliver the sacred calendar and ritual knowledge to a people, then withdraw — Tages sinks into the furrow, Quetzalcoatl departs over the sea.
Sky-father storm-wielders of unrelated families converging on the same joint, both defined by the thunderbolt at the head of their pantheons.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly formalized, codified system for reading the intentions of the gods to navigate destiny and avert disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both demand exact, unforgiving spatial and ritual orientation to correctly interface with the unseen and avoid disaster.
GOLD SEAM: Complex, geometric grid-maps used to codify and invoke specific divine intelligences mapped strictly to spatial and directional zones.
GOLD SEAM: A hyper-technical, mathematically rigid system of determining the will and location of divine forces based on intense spatial orientation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are prophetic bringers of a highly technical, esoteric divinatory science that forms the bedrock of a closed priestly system.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent attempts to codify and systematize an indigenous polytheistic worldview into a concrete framework for correct behavior and ritual procedure.
GOLD SEAM: Highly systematized, geographically mapped ritual sciences that encode the entire theological worldview into exact procedures.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the powerful, aristocratic feminine divine in pre-Roman Western Mediterranean cultures, often blurring with civic identity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are monstrous, terrifying funerary sentinels guarding the threshold of the underworld in neighboring pre-Roman cultures.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve complex bronze metallurgical objects acting as permanent, physical interfaces between human ritual and the divine will.
GOLD SEAM: An esoteric, divinely revealed textual corpus that provides the secret codes for interpreting the cosmos and maintaining universal balance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a comprehensive, revealed system of interpreting mundane physical data to decode the explicit will and order of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the foundational revealer of a highly structured, localized system of earth-centered ritual practice.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The conviction that the material world is encoded with a readable network of divine signatures, sympathies, and cosmic maps.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Etruscan discipline seeks to perfectly align with the will of the gods to maintain order; the Seven-Fold Way seeks to aggressively sever the initiate from all cosmic or social laws.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems use rigorous spatial geometry and temporary terrestrial marks to map and invite the presence of unseen powers.
GOLD SEAM: Youthful, earth-bound deities associated with the sudden, miraculous revelation of hidden sacred knowledge and physical healing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Rigid, highly cryptic behavioral prescriptions that translate immense cosmic anxieties into daily, hyper-specific taboos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A fearsome, heavily armed guardian of the underworld boundary who marshals the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly esoteric system of elite priestly knowledge that anchored the cosmic state, entirely lost when the priestly class collapsed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A singular, foundational prophet who dictates an exhaustive, unalterable system of law to a specific people before permanently departing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A rigidly observed, closed corpus of revealed texts that governs all acceptable interactions with the divine.
GOLD SEAM: Both transmute spiritual intervention from impassioned prayer into an exact, highly technical methodology requiring rigid adherence to protocol and the reading of instruments.
GOLD SEAM: Miraculous earthly eruptions—whether a prophetic child from the soil or blazing gold from the sky—that definitively reveal the structural discipline of the society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A highly codified, institutionalized method for reading the patterns of reality to determine the truth, swapping divination for empiricism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel—a highly formalized, institutionalized system of forecasting the future to align state and community survival with divine will.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine emissary who miraculously appears to the earthly populace specifically to impart the secret, practical arts of magic and divination.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Leland traces the Lasa directly to Etruscan underworld deities, viewing them as a surviving, analogical continuation of ancient animistic entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A formalized method of seeking direct, practical answers from the divine regarding specific, mundane human choices.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A figure who arrives to transmit a specific, functional spiritual technology to humanity, rather than demanding personal devotion.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions treat cosmic interaction as a highly technical, bureaucratic science of sacred writing and legalistic boundaries rather than ecstatic devotion.
GOLD SEAM: The prophetic figure who appears to reveal a comprehensive, codified system of rituals, cosmic laws, and divinatory practices to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A microcosm used by specialists to read the disposition of the unseen world regarding the current state of affairs.
GOLD SEAM: Both figures ostensibly appear out of nowhere to reveal a complete, intricate system of magical and religious technology to a receptive culture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A strictly bounded, divinely revealed corpus that dictates the exact spiritual and ritual order, kept esoteric and closed to outsiders.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Revelation not as an ancient book, but as direct, ongoing dictation from the divine to human scribes from the earth/sand.

dossier.essence: The religion of the Etruscans (Rasenna), the non-Indo-European-speaking people of Etruria in west-central Italy, flourishing from roughly the 8th to the 1st century BCE before their absorption into Rome. It centered on a 'revealed' science of reading the will of the gods and on scrupulous ritual exactitude; the Romans thought the Etruscans the most religious of peoples and inherited their arts of divination wholesale.
dossier.cosmos: The Etruscans held to a rigidly ordered cosmos whose divine will was legible everywhere to a trained reader: the sky was partitioned into sixteen regions apportioned to named gods, as modeled on the bronze Piacenza Liver, and history ran through a fixed number of world-ages (saecula) with a divinely allotted span for the Etruscan nation itself. Painted tombs show a developed underworld ruled by Aita and Phersipnai and attended by the native death-daemons Charun and Vanth. The elaborate funerary culture implies a deep concern with the passage and fate of the dead.
dossier.practice: Religious life was governed by the disciplina etrusca — the codified arts of the haruspex (reading entrails, above all the liver of sacrifices), the fulgural priest (interpreting lightning), and the reading of prodigies — administered by a trained priesthood and demanding exact procedure. Public, civic, and funerary ritual, sacrifice, and the very orientation of temples and cities followed these revealed rules.
dossier.canon: The sacred books of the disciplina (the libri haruspicini, fulgurales, and rituales), attributed to the revealer-child Tages and the nymph Vegoia, survive only in fragments and Roman paraphrase; the physical Piacenza Liver, the bilingual Pyrgi Tablets, and tomb inscriptions are the primary witnesses to a corpus otherwise lost.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds divination as exact science — the conviction that the gods' intentions are continuously inscribed in the world (in liver, lightning, and thunder) and are to be decoded by rule rather than merely petitioned.
sources: Nancy Thomson de Grummond, 'Etruscan Myth, Sacred History, and Legend' (2006)
Jean MacIntosh Turfa, 'Divining the Etruscan World: The Brontoscopic Calendar and Religious Practice' (2012)
Larissa Bonfante and Judith Swaddling, 'Etruscan Myths' (2006)
